critic_raw:
This design compares a DQN agent against a production heuristic routing algorithm for last-mile delivery time optimization.

The most disqualifying flaw is in Section 6: 'If the model does not perform as expected on the test data, slight parameter adjustments are permissible to ensure alignment with operational goals.' This is explicit, unambiguous test set manipulation. The test set is being used as a feedback signal for parameter adjustment, which means the reported test performance is not a held-out estimate of generalization — it is an estimate of the model's ability to fit the test set after direct optimization against it. No confidence interval or statistical test computed on this data has valid coverage.

Second, the preprocessing states that all transformers are 'fit on the combined training and validation data (months 1–12)' — which is explicitly described as intentional and the cost of potential minimal data leakage is accepted. The test set spans months 13-18. Fitting scalers and encoders on months 1-12 and applying them to months 13-18 is correct and not leakage. This is one of the few cases where the preprocessing choice is sound. However, the design mixes this correct statement with the claim that this is done to 'stabilize parameter estimates given seasonal and geographic variability' — which is not the right justification; the correct justification is preventing leakage. The confused justification suggests the authors may not distinguish the correct from incorrect preprocessing approaches across sections.

Third, the DQN agent's action space is defined as 'select from 10–20 candidate routes (generated by a fast heuristic route generator).' This means the RL agent is selecting from routes pre-generated by a heuristic, and the heuristic's quality bounds the RL agent's performance. If the candidate route generator is the same algorithm as the heuristic baseline, the RL agent cannot outperform the baseline on any route not already in the candidate set — the comparison becomes whether the RL agent's selection among pre-generated routes outperforms random or simple selection among the same routes. This is a much weaker claim than 'RL outperforms heuristic routing.'

Fourth, the reward function penalizes customer satisfaction drops: '−5 × max(0, 4.5 − customer_satisfaction_rating).' Customer satisfaction ratings are collected post-delivery and are not available at routing decision time. If the reward function uses actual satisfaction ratings during RL training, the agent is trained with information unavailable at deployment — which is temporal leakage in the reward signal itself.
all_issues_raised: Section 6 explicitly permits parameter adjustments if test set performance is unsatisfactory; this invalidates the test set as a held-out estimate and makes all reported confidence intervals and statistical tests meaningless.

The RL agent selects from candidate routes pre-generated by the same class of heuristic used as the baseline, meaning the agent cannot discover routes superior to those generated by the heuristic; the comparison measures selection quality among heuristic-generated routes, not whether RL discovers better routing strategies.

Customer satisfaction ratings are collected post-delivery but are included in the RL training reward function as if available at decision time; this is temporal leakage in the reward signal where training-time rewards use outcomes that would not be available in deployment.

The preprocessing justification conflates stability of parameter estimates with leakage prevention; this conceptual confusion suggests the design may have other unreported preprocessing decisions that are incorrectly framed as stability improvements rather than recognized as leakage.
all_issues_adjudicated: Section 6 explicitly permits parameter adjustments if test set performance is unsatisfactory; this invalidates the test set as a held-out estimate and makes all reported confidence intervals and statistical tests meaningless.

The RL agent selects from candidate routes pre-generated by the same class of heuristic used as the baseline, meaning the agent cannot discover routes superior to those generated by the heuristic; the comparison measures selection quality among heuristic-generated routes, not whether RL discovers better routing strategies.

Customer satisfaction ratings are collected post-delivery but are included in the RL training reward function as if available at decision time; this is temporal leakage in the reward signal where training-time rewards use outcomes that would not be available in deployment.

The preprocessing justification conflates stability of parameter estimates with leakage prevention; this conceptual confusion suggests the design may have other unreported preprocessing decisions that are incorrectly framed as stability improvements rather than recognized as leakage.
